text: Photonic computing is considered as a promising approach for fast, power-efficient vision processing. However, photonic computing is held back by optical nonlinearity, ADC power, and noise resilience. In this work, we design and implement an all-analog chip ACCEL (optical–electrical Accelerator for Computer vision using Light) that integrates the electronic and photonic processing units to address the challenges in photonic computing. Our design achieves a system level power efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt (PetaOPS/W) with processing throughput of 4.6 PetaOPS while achieving over 99% optical processing with three orders of magnitude improvement in power efficiency and one order of magnitude improvement in processing throughput when compared with state-of-the-art computing systems. The photocurrent generated due to feature encoding using diffractive optics is processed within an integrated analog circuit without the need of converting them into digital signals using analog-to-digital converters. This leads to a frame latency of only 72 ns. Through joint optimization of photonic–electronic hardware and the learning algorithm, the system achieves experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% on Fashion-MNIST, 82% on 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% on time-lapse video classification. The system is highly resilient under ultra low light conditions of 0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame. In summary, our system demonstrates the potential of ACCEL in various power-constrained, high-throughput applications including that of wearable electronics, self-driving cars, and industrial inspection systems.
